figures.gloss: The radical equivalence of God and the natural universe. God is not a creator who stands outside of existence, but the infinite, eternal, and necessary reality of existence itself, identical with the laws of physics and the cosmos.
figures.facet: It destroys the transcendent, personal deity entirely. God has no will, no purpose, no judgments, and no emotional attachments; God is simply the totality of what is, demanding understanding rather than worship.

figures.gloss: The single, indivisible foundation of all reality. Everything that exists—mind, matter, galaxies, and thoughts—are merely transient modifications or 'modes' of this singular, infinite essence.
figures.facet: It abolishes the Cartesian mind-body divide. Mind and matter are not separate substances, but parallel attributes of the exact same underlying reality, forever entwined and fundamentally identical.

figures.gloss: Nature in its active, generative, and infinite capacity. It is the uncaused cause, the self-generating engine of existence operating purely through the necessity of its own eternal laws.
figures.facet: It completely replaces 'creation ex nihilo' (creation out of nothing). Reality was never authored at a point in time; it is ceaselessly and necessarily unfolding from its own infinite nature.
figures.provenance: Spinoza's 'Ethics', borrowing from Medieval Scholasticism but radically secularized.

figures.gloss: The passive, manifested universe. It is the totality of all specific, finite things—individual organisms, planets, thoughts, and objects—that result inevitably from the laws of Natura Naturans.
figures.facet: It posits absolute determinism. Every single mode—every human action, thought, and physical event—is strictly determined by preceding causes, leaving absolutely no room for human free will.

figures.gloss: The innate, striving force by which everything endeavors to persist in its own being. For rocks, it is inertia; for living things, it is the drive to survive; for humans, it is desire and self-preservation.
figures.facet: It grounds all ethics in biological and physical reality. Good and evil are not absolute cosmic laws, but merely subjective human labels for what aids or obstructs our specific 'conatus'.
figures.provenance: Spinoza's 'Ethics'; parallels in Thomas Hobbes and modern evolutionary biology.

figures.gloss: The 'intellectual love of God'—the highest human joy, achieved by rationally comprehending the necessity of all things and our precise place within the infinite web of Nature.
figures.facet: It is a love that expects no love in return. God/Nature has no emotions, so to love God is simply to accept the universe exactly as it is, without demanding it care for you.

figures.gloss: The Earth understood as a complex, self-regulating, and synergistic organism. The biosphere, atmosphere, oceans, and soils interact mechanically and chemically to maintain optimal conditions for life to persist.
figures.facet: It resurrects the ancient Earth Goddess as a purely scientific, cybernetic system. It requires no teleology or conscious intent; life regulates the planet blindly but effectively through feedback loops.
figures.provenance: James Lovelock and Lynn Margulis's 'Gaia Hypothesis' (1970s).

figures.gloss: The deep ecological principle that all biological life is fundamentally entangled. Organisms have no isolated existence; their reality is entirely defined by their relationships within the broader ecosystem.
figures.facet: It forces a radical biocentrism, completely displacing human beings from the apex of creation. A human is merely one strand in the web, possessing no greater inherent right to exist than a microbe.
figures.provenance: Arne Naess's 'Deep Ecology' (1973); modern environmental pantheism.

figures.gloss: Amsterdam lens-grinder (1632-1677), excommunicated (cherem) from his Sephardic community at 23, whose geometric-method philosophy in the Ethics identified God with Nature and grounded the tradition the roster is otherwise built entirely on citations of.
figures.facet: The tradition's founder is the one figure the pre-existing roster names constantly in its provenance fields yet never rosters itself — every principle here is a proposition of his system, not a free-floating idea.
figures.provenance: Biographical consensus (Steven Nadler, Spinoza: A Life, 1999); Spinoza's own Ethics (1677).

figures.gloss: Spinoza's posthumously published masterwork (1677), proving in Euclidean definitions, axioms, and propositions the existence of a single infinite substance, its necessary unfolding into all finite things, and the path to human blessedness through understanding it.
figures.facet: The tradition's founding scripture takes the form of a geometry textbook rather than a revelation: its authority rests on demonstration, not testimony, which is itself the tradition's defining claim about how truth about God can be known.

figures.gloss: Irish freethinker (1670-1722) who coined 'pantheist' in print in Socinianism Truly Stated (1705) and sketched an actual pantheist liturgy and society of adherents in the Pantheisticon (1720).
figures.facet: Gave the tradition its name and its first attempt at communal form a generation after Spinoza's death, turning a condemned philosophical position into a namable religious stance.
figures.provenance: John Toland, Socinianism Truly Stated (1705) and Pantheisticon (1720).

figures.gloss: Dominican-turned-heretic (1548-1600), burned at the stake at Campo de' Fiori in Rome, who taught an infinite universe of innumerable inhabited worlds animated throughout by a single divine immanence, decades before Spinoza.
figures.facet: The tradition's proto-martyr, canonized retroactively: his execution long predates Spinoza's system and was for heresies broader than pantheism specifically, but 19th-century pantheists claimed him as their first casualty.
figures.provenance: Standard biographical/historical accounts of Bruno's trial and execution (1600); his De l'infinito, universo e mondi (1584).

figures.gloss: Spinoza's third and highest kind of knowledge: perceiving each particular thing as a necessary consequence of God/Nature's essence, 'under the aspect of eternity' rather than as an event located in duration.
figures.facet: The epistemic technique that completes Amor Dei Intellectualis: one does not merely accept Nature's necessity abstractly but learns to see each concrete thing, here and now, as timelessly entailed by it.

figures.gloss: The German 'pantheism controversy' of 1785-89: Friedrich Jacobi's public disclosure that Gotthold Lessing had privately confessed Spinozism forced Moses Mendelssohn, Herder, and Goethe to publicly stake out positions on Spinoza.
figures.facet: The historical hinge by which pantheism passed from a nearly unmentionable heresy-label into a live, respectable position among Europe's leading intellectuals, explaining the tradition's Romantic-era peak.
figures.provenance: Friedrich Heinrich Jacobi, Über die Lehre des Spinoza in Briefen an den Herrn Moses Mendelssohn (1785); standard intellectual-historical accounts of the controversy.

figures.gloss: Albert Einstein's 1929 telegraphed reply to Rabbi Herbert Goldstein — 'I believe in Spinoza's God, who reveals himself in the lawful harmony of all that exists, not in a God who concerns himself with the fates and actions of human beings' — since adopted as the emblematic modern credo of scientific pantheism and religious naturalism.
figures.facet: The bridge from Spinoza's 17th-century geometric God to the tradition's living, science-facing wing: the same impersonal, law-identified divinity restated in the vocabulary of physics rather than metaphysics.
figures.provenance: Einstein's 1929 telegram to Rabbi Herbert S. Goldstein, widely quoted in Einstein biographies (e.g. Walter Isaacson, Einstein: His Life and Universe, 2007).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: They are the active and passive faces of the exact same infinite reality.
Intra-tradition bond: Substance is the ontological structure; Deus sive Natura is the theological realization of it.
Intra-tradition bond: Amor Dei Intellectualis is the highest, most rational expression of human conatus striving for empowerment.
Intra-tradition bond: Gaia is the macro-organism, while the Web of Life describes its internal, interdependent mechanics.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Spinoza explicitly redefined the Abrahamic Yahweh into a mechanistic, non-personal universe, triggering his excommunication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lovelock explicitly named his scientific hypothesis after the Greek Earth Goddess, syncretizing myth into cybernetic ecology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a single, infinite, all-encompassing reality that constitutes the entirety of existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both describe a single, dynamic energy that is identical to the cosmos itself, constantly generating reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the uncaused, spontaneous generative force that effortlessly produces the myriad things.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both signify the infinite, unknowable, and limitless foundation from which all finite attributes emanate.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both describe the absolute, indivisible singularity from which all varied existence is formed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize an inherent, irreducible life-force or striving essence fundamentally present within entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Spinoza's realization that the individual self is merely a transient mode aligns with the Buddhist dissolution of the ego into the interdependent whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent ultimate liberation achieved through perfect, unclouded understanding of reality's true nature.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision the animating force not as a personified ruler, but as the great generative essence running through all things.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both view the Earth not as dead matter to be exploited, but as a living entity demanding respect and maintaining ecological balance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize the planet as a total, self-sustaining living system that integrates all biological and geological processes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both frame reality as an intricate system of balance, where disrupting one strand violently disrupts the harmony of the whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both visualize the unbreakable circle of interdependence linking all forms of life and natural phenomena.
Intra-tradition: the philosopher and the single work that contains his entire system.
Intra-tradition: the Ethics is the textual proof-structure from which Deus sive Natura is demonstrated, not merely asserted.
Intra-tradition: Bruno's infinite, divinity-suffused cosmos predates Spinoza's system by decades and was retroactively read by 19th-century pantheists as its forerunner and first martyr.
Intra-tradition: Toland's Pantheisticon draws directly on Spinozist metaphysics in coining and liturgizing the term 'pantheist' a generation after Spinoza's death.
Intra-tradition: the controversy is explicitly a dispute over Lessing's, and by extension Spinoza's, legacy and respectability.
Intra-tradition: Einstein names Spinoza directly and explicitly in the source quotation — a documented citation, not an inferred resemblance.
Intra-tradition: Einstein's 'lawful harmony of all that exists' restates Deus sive Natura in the register of 20th-century physics.
Intra-tradition: the epistemic stance (seeing under the aspect of eternity) that completes the affective state (the intellectual love of God) — Ethics Part V treats them as one movement.

Cross-tradition: Bruno's cosmology is argued by historians (notably Frances Yates) to be deeply infused with Hermetic thought, though the extent of that influence versus his Neoplatonist and Lullian sources remains debated — kept at analogous/tier 3 rather than syncretic given that debate.

dossier.essence: A modern Western philosophical-religious stance, and a loose family of naturalistic spiritualities, that identifies God with the totality of nature or the cosmos rather than with a transcendent person. Its touchstone is Baruch Spinoza's 'Ethics' (1677) and its formula 'Deus sive Natura' (God, or Nature); the word 'pantheism' derives from John Toland early in the 18th century, and the stance now extends to Romantic nature-religion and to contemporary eco-spiritual forms (Gaianism, the World Pantheist Movement). It is a living self-identification rather than a single church.
dossier.cosmos: On the Spinozan model there is one infinite substance, Nature, of which mind and matter are two attributes and all finite things are transient 'modes'; it acts by strict necessity, without will, purpose, providence, or creation from nothing. Personal immortality in the ordinary sense falls away (Spinoza's 'eternity of the mind' is intellectual, not survival). Gaian and deep-ecological versions recast this immanent whole as a self-regulating living Earth or an interdependent web of life.
dossier.practice: Practice is primarily intellectual and ethical rather than cultic: for Spinoza, freedom and blessedness come through the 'intellectual love of God' (amor Dei intellectualis) — understanding nature's necessity and so achieving equanimity. Modern pantheist and naturalist bodies (such as the World Pantheist Movement, founded 1999) cultivate reverence for nature, contemplative appreciation, and an environmental ethic, largely without clergy or sacraments.
dossier.canon: Spinoza's 'Ethics' (1677) is the foundational text; the wider stance draws on Romantic and modern nature-writing and, for its Gaian wing, on the scientific literature of the Gaia hypothesis rather than on any scripture.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds reverence without a personal God — the religious impulse redirected from a transcendent deity to the immanent totality of nature, so that worship gives way to understanding.
sources: Michael P. Levine, 'Pantheism: A Non-Theistic Concept of Deity' (1994)
Jonathan I. Israel, 'Radical Enlightenment: Philosophy and the Making of Modernity' (2001)
